meta.title: One Year Bible Plan iPhone: The Setup That Survives Past February
meta.description: A one year bible plan iphone setup that actually finishes — which plan to pick, the ~15-minute daily math, and how to keep the next reading impossible to forget.

sections.title: Quick Answer
sections.content: The most reliable **one year Bible plan iPhone** setup pairs two things: a reading-plan app (YouVersion's Bible in a Year, a chronological plan, or the classic M'Cheyne) and a way to keep today's reading visible — a home-screen widget or a verse from the day on your lock screen. Pick a plan you can finish in about 15 minutes a day, and make the next reading impossible to forget.

sections.text: Almost everyone who starts a **one year Bible plan iPhone** setup starts strong. Genesis is gripping. Exodus holds up. Then Leviticus arrives in mid-February, you miss two days, the catch-up pile grows, and by March the plan is a checkbox you avoid opening. The plan didn't fail because it was too hard. It failed because it went out of sight.

Reading the whole Bible in a year is genuinely doable — it's roughly **3.3 chapters a day**, about 12 to 15 minutes of reading. The problem was never the volume. It's that a plan living inside an app you forget to open is a plan that quietly disappears.
Pick a plan you can actually finish
Not all one-year plans are equal. The order you read in changes whether you make it to December. Three that hold up:

items: **Chronological** — read events in the order they happened. The narrative pull keeps momentum better than front-to-back.
**Old + New each day** — a few chapters of each daily, so you're never stuck in one hard stretch (Leviticus goes down easier next to a Gospel).
**M'Cheyne** — the classic 19th-century plan: four readings a day, the whole Bible once and the New Testament and Psalms twice. Ambitious but balanced.

sections.content: If you've stalled out before, **don't pick straight Genesis-to-Revelation.** The canonical order front-loads law and genealogy. A chronological or OT/NT-split plan is far more likely to carry you past the February wall.

sections.text: The iPhone setup, step by step

items: Open **YouVersion** (free) and start a plan — search "Bible in a Year" or "Chronological" and pick your translation.
Set the plan's daily **reminder** for a fixed time you already have margin — right after coffee, on the commute, before bed.
Add the **reading-plan widget** to your home screen so today's passage shows without opening anything.
Pick one verse from each day to carry — set it on your **lock screen** so the reading follows you into the day.
Turn off the daily push notification *only* if the widget and lock screen are doing the reminding. One visible cue beats three you swipe away.

sections.text: One more decision matters more than people expect: your translation. A plan you can read fluently is a plan you'll finish, so don't pick the most academic version out of guilt. The ESV, CSB, and NIV all read smoothly at a daily pace; if the language slows you down, switch. Twelve months with a translation you actually understand beats two months with one you respect but slog through.

sections.label: Average daily iPhone pickups
sections.context: per Reviews.org's 2026 Phone Habits report — every one a chance to be reminded of today's reading, if it's on a surface you actually see

sections.text: But his delight is in the law of the LORD, and on his law he meditates day and night.

Here's the failure mode no plan app warns you about. The plan lives inside the app. The app lives in a folder. The folder lives on page three of your home screen. So the entire **one year Bible plan iPhone** habit depends on you remembering to open something you've buried. Miss two days and the catch-up guilt makes you avoid it further — and a plan you're avoiding is a plan that's already over.

This is the gap **FaithWall** closes. It isn't the reading plan — it's the part that keeps the plan in front of you. You pick a verse from today's passage, and it sits on your lock screen, rotating automatically through iOS Focus modes. You don't open an app to be reminded; the reminder is already there, 144 times a day. The reading happens once. The staying-with-it happens every time you glance down.

sections.content: A reading plan is the **input** — your 15 minutes in the Word. A lock-screen verse is **retention** — keeping a line of it in view until the next day's reading. Different jobs. The people who actually reach Revelation in December tend to run both.

**The grace rule:** build catch-up into the plan, not on top of it. Take one day a week — Sunday works — with *no* assigned reading. Miss a day? You're not behind; you fill it Sunday. This single rule rescues more plans than any app feature.

sections.text: For the widget mechanics that keep the plan glanceable, [the bible reading plan widget iphone guide](/blog/bible-reading-plan-widget-iphone) goes deeper. To pick the reading app itself, [the best free bible app iphone breakdown](/blog/best-free-bible-app-iphone) compares YouVersion and Olive Tree, and [how to highlight verses on iphone](/blog/how-to-highlight-verses-on-iphone) makes each day's reading stick. For the full toolkit, see the [Bible study tools pillar guide](/bible-study-tools-ios).

sections.title: Keep the plan in view — let FaithWall carry the verse
sections.description: Free, 60 seconds, no account. FaithWall keeps a verse from today's reading on your lock screen through iOS Focus modes — the visibility layer that gets you to December.
